name: Galaico-Portuguese

ancestors.notes: Primary descent from Hispanic Vulgar Latin. The NW Iberian development of spoken Latin.
Gallaecian Celtic substrate: strongest in NW Iberia. Castro culture, toponyms, possible influence on nasal vowel development.
Lusitanian substrate: pre-Celtic IE language of western Iberia. Possible influence on /v/≠/b/ preservation.
Suebi (Germanic) superstrate in Gallaecia (411–585 CE): Kingdom of the Suebi. ~200 place names, vocabulary (guerra, roubar, branco).
Visigothic superstrate shared with all Ibero-Romance.
Occitan adstrate: nh and lh digraphs inherited from occitan
Mozarabic adstrate: less intense in the NW than in central/southern Iberia due to shorter Moorish presence.
Andalusi Arabic adstrate: less intense in the NW than in central/southern Iberia due to shorter Moorish presence.

notes: Galaico-Portuguese (~9th–14th c. CE). The shared ancestor of modern Galician and Portuguese. Attested in troubadour lyric poetry (Cantigas de Santa Maria, cantigas de amigo, cantigas de amor — >1,600 texts), early legal documents (Notícia de Torto ~1214, Testamento de D. Afonso II ~1214). KEY FEATURES: (1) Medieval 4-way sibilant contrast: /ts/ ⟨ç⟩ vs /dz/ ⟨z⟩ vs /s/ ⟨ss⟩ vs /z/ ⟨-s-⟩. (2) /ʎ/ ⟨lh⟩, /ɲ/ ⟨nh⟩ digraph convention from Occitan. (3) /b/ ≠ /v/ preserved. (4) F- preserved (filho, not hijo). (5) /ou/ still a true diphthong. (6) Nasal vowels developing but not fully phonemicised. The split into Galician and Portuguese is conventionally dated to ~mid-14th c. when the Portuguese court settled in Lisbon and Galician fell under Castilian influence. Source: Maia (1986), Castro (2006), Teyssier (1982).
